message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 found the world in a state of unpreparedness. While research efforts to develop a vaccine are on-going, others have suggested the use of available vaccines to boost innate immunity.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We analysed three databases: UNICEF Immunization Coverage, Worldometer Corona Virus Updates and World Bank List of Economies to establish the association, if any, between vaccination for various diseases and COVID-19 death rates and recoveries across world economies.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Mean percentage death rates were lower in countries that vaccinated for Hepatitis-B birth dose (2.53% vs 3.79%, p = 0.001), Bacille Calmette-Guérin Vaccine (2.93% vs 5.10%, p = 0.025) and Inactivated Polio Vaccine 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    dose (2.8% vs 4.01%, p = 0.022) than those which did not report vaccination. In high income countries, a significant negative correlation with death rates was observed with vaccination for Measles-containing vaccine 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    dose (r = –0.290, p = 0.032), Rubella-containing vaccine 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    dose (r = –0.325, p = 0.015), Hepatitis B 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    dose (r = –0.562, p = 3.3 x10
                    <jats:sup>−5</jats:sup>
                    ), Inactivated Polio vaccine 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    dose (r = –0.720, p = 0.008). Inactivated Polio Vaccine 1st dose and Measles-containing vaccine 2
                    <jats:sup>nd</jats:sup>
                    dose also correlated with better recoveries. In Low Income countries, only Rubella-containing vaccine correlated with lower deaths while Yellow fever vaccine was associated with poorer recoveries.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our analysis corroborates the potential benefit of vaccination and warrant further research to explore the rationale for repurposing other vaccines to fight COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Vaccination for some childhood diseases may impact the outcome of covid-19 infections

reference.unstructured: Miller A , Reandelar MJ , Fasciglione K , Roumenova V , Li Y , Otazu GH . Correlation between universal BCG vaccination policy and reduced morbidity and mortality for COVID-19: an epidemiological study. MedRxiv. 2020.

reference.article-title: Could BCG be used to protect against COVID-19?
Long-lasting effects of BCG vaccination on both heterologous Th1/Th17 responses and innate trained immunity

reference.unstructured: Singh S . BCG vaccines may not reduce COVID-19 mortality rates. medRxiv. 2020:2020.04.11.20062232.

reference.article-title: Considering BCG vaccination to reduce the impact of COVID-19

reference.unstructured: Hegarty PK , Kamat AM , Zafirakis H , Dinardo A . BCG vaccination may be protective against Covid-19. preprint. 2020.

Worldometer: Corona Virus updates [Internet]. 2020 [cited 03 August 2020]. Available from: https://www.worldometers.info/.
